coredata.dc:title: Sonolysis of Albunex®-supplemented, 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes by pulsed 1-MHz ultrasound: pulse number, pulse duration and exposure vessel rotation dependence
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 1999 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Brayman, Andrew A
Miller, Morton W
coredata.dc:description: The hypotheses tested were that sonolysis of erythrocytes in the presence of a gas-based ultrasound contrast agent in vitro will be related quantitatively to the duration and number of ultrasound pulses applied using a constant pulse repetition period and, at least qualitatively, to the total exposure duration (i.e., the product of pulse number × pulse duration). An objective was to determine the influence of sample rotation during insonation on the amount of hemolysis produced under these conditions. Human erythrocytes, suspended to 40% hematocrit in autologous plasma containing 3.6% (V:V) Albunex®, were exposed/sham-exposed to 1–100 pulses of 1-MHz ultrasound (6.2 MPa peak positive, 3.6 MPa peak negative acoustic pressures; ISPTP ∼ 800 W/cm2) using a 1-s pulse repetition period. Pulse durations ranged from 20–20,000 μs; samples were either stationary or rotated (200 rpm) during insonation. Hemolysis was independent of vessel rotation treatment at all tested pulse durations and pulse numbers. Levels of hemolysis statistically greater than in sham-exposed samples were obtained with ≥50 pulses of 20 μs duration, and ≥1 pulse of 200, 2000 or 20,000 μs duration. Hemolysis increased with increasing pulse number and pulse duration. Approximately equivalent levels of hemolysis were produced by different pulse number × pulse duration combinations, yielding the same total exposure duration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Albunex®
Ultrasound bioeffects
Acoustic cavitation
Hemolysis
Pulsed ultrasound

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Background-corrected hemolysis as a function of the number of applied 1-MHz ultrasound pulses of (A) 20, (B) 200, (C) 2000 or (D) 20,000 μs durations. Filled symbols represent experimental observations obtained using stationary exposure vessels; open circles represent experimental observations acquired with 200 rpm sample rotation. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean.

Background-corrected hemolysis as a function of 1-MHz ultrasound pulse duration (20–20,000 μs) at applied pulse numbers ranging from 1–100. The inset panel provides a more detailed view of the data obtained with a single pulse. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean.

Background-corrected hemolysis as a function of total “on” time of the various pulse duration × pulse number treatment combinations. The inset panel provides an expanded view of the data. Error bars represent the standard error of the mean.
Hemolysis of 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes in autologous plasma produced by 1–100 pulses of 1-MHz ultrasound delivered at a 1-Hz pulse-repetition frequencylegend

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: legend
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Pulse durations ranged from 20–20,000 μs. All samples contained 3.6% (V:V) Albunex® and were rotated at 200 rpm during insonation. Parenthetical values represent the p value obtained with one-sided t tests of Ha:Expt’l > 0; NS represents a p value > 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hemolysis of 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes in autologous plasma produced by 1–100 pulses of 1-MHz ultrasound delivered at a 1-Hz pulse-repetition frequency.legend

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: legend
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Pulse durations ranged from 20–20,000 μs. All samples contained 3.6% (V:V) Albunex® and were stationary during insonation. Parenthetical values represent the p value obtained with one-sided t tests of Ha:Expt’l > 0; NS represents a p value > 0.05.

head.ce:title: Sonolysis of Albunex®-supplemented, 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes by pulsed 1-MHz ultrasound: pulse number, pulse duration and exposure vessel rotation dependence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, School of Medicine and Dentistry, The University of Rochester, 601 Elmwood Ave., Rochester, NY 14642 USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address correspondence to: Dr. A. A. Brayman, University of Rochester, School of Medicine & Dentistry, Dept of Obstetrics and Gynecology, 601 Elmwood Ave., Rochester, NY 14642

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hypotheses tested were that sonolysis of erythrocytes in the presence of a gas-based ultrasound contrast agent in vitro will be related quantitatively to the duration and number of ultrasound pulses applied using a constant pulse repetition period and, at least qualitatively, to the total exposure duration (i.e., the product of pulse number × pulse duration). An objective was to determine the influence of sample rotation during insonation on the amount of hemolysis produced under these conditions. Human erythrocytes, suspended to 40% hematocrit in autologous plasma containing 3.6% (V:V) Albunex®, were exposed/sham-exposed to 1–100 pulses of 1-MHz ultrasound (6.2 MPa peak positive, 3.6 MPa peak negative acoustic pressures; ISPTP ∼ 800 W/cm2) using a 1-s pulse repetition period. Pulse durations ranged from 20–20,000 μs; samples were either stationary or rotated (200 rpm) during insonation. Hemolysis was independent of vessel rotation treatment at all tested pulse durations and pulse numbers. Levels of hemolysis statistically greater than in sham-exposed samples were obtained with ≥50 pulses of 20 μs duration, and ≥1 pulse of 200, 2000 or 20,000 μs duration. Hemolysis increased with increasing pulse number and pulse duration. Approximately equivalent levels of hemolysis were produced by different pulse number × pulse duration combinations, yielding the same total exposure duration.

ce:section.ce:para: The “potentiation,” or enhancement of ultrasound-induced bioeffects by gas-based contrast agents is a relatively new area of investigation, but one of considerable interest because of the continued development and clinical applications of such agents. In nearly all cases, the increase in bioeffect extent associated with the coincident presence of contrast agent and ultrasound exposure can be attributed to cavitation effects, particularly to the effects of inertial cavitation. Albunex® (Holland and Apfel 1990) and other gas-based contrast agents (Miller and Thomas 1995) markedly decrease the acoustic pressure thresholds for inertial cavitation activity in aqueous media. Such agents have also been shown to increase the extent of ultrasound-induced bioeffects relative to those in materials lacking contrast agents, both in vitro (Williams et al. 1991; M. W. Miller et al. 1995; Brayman et al. 1995, Brayman et al.1996a, Brayman et al.1996b, Brayman et al.1997; Miller and Thomas 1996; D. L. Miller et al. 1997; Miller and Geis 1998a; Miller and Bao 1998) and in vivo (Dalecki 1997a, Dalecki 1997b; Miller and Geis 1998b). Everbach et al. (1997) have demonstrated a correlation between sonolytic damage to human erythrocytes and a concomitant physical measure of inertial cavitation activity.

The acoustic pressure, center frequency of the applied ultrasound, pulse duration, and the extent of nucleation of the insonated fluid influence the occurrence of inertial cavitation. For example, Holland and Apfel (1990) determined that the peak negative acoustic pressure threshold for inertial cavitation in highly filtered water was ∼2.3 MPa at 1 MHz. At a slightly lower frequency (0.76 MHz), the cavitation threshold for saline solution containing soluble albumin was similar (∼1.6 MPa); the threshold was much lower (∼0.5–0.6 MPa) when Albunex® microbubbles were substituted for the albumin. Pulse duration also influences cavitation activity. The Flynn and Church (1988) analysis indicates that, over the acoustic frequency range of 0.1 to 5 MHz, greater inertial cavitation activity is achieved with longer pulse durations. This suggests that, at comparable acoustic pressures and duty factors, the greater the pulse duration, the greater will be the bioeffect(s) produced. The greater biological effectiveness of continuous-wave (CW) ultrasound exposures, relative to pulsed ultrasound exposures of the same total “on” duration and constant pressure amplitude (Miller and Geis 1998a), is consistent with this idea. At seeming variance is that Brayman et al. (1997) reported a weak pulse duration-dependence of ultrasound-induced hemolysis of whole human blood in vitro, with only slightly greater hemolysis attending a 10-fold increase in pulse duration at comparable ultrasound frequencies and pressure amplitudes. In those experiments, the pulse durations were either 20 or 200 μs, with a constant duty factor of 0.01 and a constant treatment time (60 s); thus, the total exposure duration (i.e., “on” time of 0.6 s) experienced by the samples was the same for both pulse-duration regimens. Nonetheless, theory indicates that the increase in inertial cavitation activity accompanying increasing pulse duration is associated with the involvement of bubbles whose initial radii cover a broader range than those activated over the shorter intervals. Flynn and Church (1988) noted that, with appropriately sized nuclei, inertial cavitation activity can occur over as little as one acoustic cycle; these nuclei were defined as “prompt nuclei.” It is clear, however, that nuclei which are not of the proper size for “promptness” may, nonetheless, undergo inertial activation after exposure to additional acoustic cycles (i.e., with acoustic “pumping”). Additionally, Flynn and Church (1988) showed that maximal bubble pulsation activity occurred with the application of ∼20 acoustic cycles, and that bubble pulsation amplitude attained an approximately steady state after ∼60 acoustic cycles (i.e., pulses longer than ∼60 acoustic cycles are roughly equivalent to CW exposures in terms of bubble pulsation activity). Although the Flynn and Church study dealt with nonencapsulated microbubbles, rather than with an encapsulated microbubble contrast agent as used here, there is evidence that ultrasonic modification of encapsulated contrast agent microbubbles produces “free” microbubbles which are capable of nucleating inertial cavitation (see below). Thus, for long pulse durations (e.g., 100–20,000 acoustic cycles), and neglecting bubble dissolution effects during the quiescent interpulse periods, one might expect that cavitation-related bioeffects would scale with the cumulative total “on” time of an ultrasound exposure.

The center frequency of the applied ultrasound also influences inertial cavitation activity, so that greater acoustic pressures are required to induce inertial cavitation at higher frequencies than at lower frequencies. Holland and Apfel (1990) and Flynn and Church (1988) are but a few who have noted the diminution of inertial cavitation activity with increasing acoustic frequency at comparable acoustic pressures. Brayman et al. 1996c, Brayman et al. 1997) and Miller and Thomas (1993) have noted that ultrasound-induced hemolysis in vitro decreases as frequency increases under otherwise comparable exposure conditions.

The gas body nucleation state of the insonated medium is another important factor. As mentioned, gas-based contrast agents have a large effect on the pressure thresholds for inertial cavitation activity. Shelled, air-based agents, such as Albunex®, are susceptible to rapid destruction by either static pressures similar to those produced by the heart (Brayman and Miller 1997), or diagnostic ultrasound acoustic pressures (Mor-Avi et al. 1994; Vandenberg and Melton 1994), and yet retain their ability to nucleate cavitation activity after several hours in the body (Dalecki et al. 1997b) or after the bulk of the original agent has been destroyed by ultrasound treatment (Brayman and Miller 1997). After an air-based contrast agent microbubble has been modified by an applied ultrasonic field, the destabilized gas body may participate in cavitation activity, or may dissolve rapidly. Miller and Geis (1998a) report that, at very high air-based contrast-agent microbubble concentrations, greater ultrasound-induced hemolysis is produced by CW exposures than by pulsed exposures of the same “on” time, a result consistent with the formation of “secondary microbubbles” upon inertial collapse of an initial gas body (Apfel 1981) and their subsequent proliferation under CW or long pulse conditions (Miller and Williams 1989, 1992). It is, however, experimentally difficult to know whether a cavitation event, observed whenever there is more than one acoustic cycle applied to a test fluid, is the result of the collapse of: 1. an initially-present bubble, or 2. a new bubble, formed from the shattering of an initial bubble. However, Miller and Geis (1998a) have produced data which suggest an answer to this question. Blood cells were exposed to 10 μs pulses of 2.4-MHz ultrasound using a 1-ms pulse repetition period (PRP) and very high concentrations of either Albunex® or a perfluorocarbon-based agent (FS069); in both cases, hemolysis increased with increasing total exposure duration. However, for total exposures >0.1 s, hemolysis in the Albunex®-supplemented preparations became relatively insensitive to further increases in exposure duration, whereas hemolysis in the FS069-supplemented preparations continued to increase markedly with increasing total exposure time. This result suggests that “secondary” gas bodies may contribute significantly to cavitation activity produced by exposure of the contrast agent to ultrasound. However, they also suggest that after a shelled, air-filled microbubble contrast agent is acoustically modified, the gas body dissolves rapidly unless acted upon by CW or long pulse-duration ultrasound, and that the greater hemolytic effectiveness of the perfluorocarbon-based agent at longer exposure durations is related to the low solubility of the perfluorocarbon gas and, thus, the persistence of perfluorocarbon gas nuclei from one acoustic pulse to the next.

The a priori hypotheses tested in this project were that hemolysis in whole human blood supplemented with Albunex® would increase: 1. with increasing pulse duration at constant pulse number; 2. with increasing pulse number at constant pulse duration; and 3. in at least a general fashion with the total duration of sample exposure to ultrasound (i.e., the product of pulse number × pulse duration). Pulses of 1-MHz ultrasound ranging from 20–20,000 μs in duration were applied at 1-s PRP over treatment times of 1–100 s. Although the dissolution of cavitation nuclei derived from acoustically-modified, air-based contrast agents seems to be an important determinant of the extent of sonolysis, even with PRPs as short as 1 ms (Miller and Geis 1998a), the ultrasound pulses applied here were relatively long (20–20,000 μs) and the PRP of 1 s was constant throughout all of the experiments. The quiescent interpulse period, during which the dissolution of unstabilized microbubbles is expected to occur, thus differed by 2% between the 20- and 20,000-μs pulse-duration treatments (i.e., was essentially the same throughout). It was, therefore, expected that approximately equivalent levels of hemolysis would be produced by exposure of samples to the same “on” time when markedly different pulse durations were used. The present data are consistent with this expectation.

Whole blood was drawn daily from adult volunteers and anticoagulated with CPDA-1 (8.4 mL/60 mL whole blood). The blood was centrifuged (10,000 g × 10 min), the plasma removed, the buffy coat removed from the red cell pellet, and the red cells resuspended in autologous plasma to an hematocrit of ∼40%. The stock cell suspension was oxygenated with O2 and maintained on ice while awaiting experimental use.

Dialysis tubing/stainless-steel exposure vessels were used. These have been described and illustrated elsewhere (M. W. Miller et al. 1995). Briefly, the exposure vessels are comprised of stainless steel and dialysis membrane (SpectroPor®; MW cutoff = 6000–8000) components. The sample is contained in a cylindrical section of dialysis membrane (volume ∼500 μL; diameter = 6.4 mm, height = 15 mm); this sonolucent “window” is bounded by a stainless-steel plug at the bottom and a stainless-steel tube at the top, through which sample loading/unloading occurs. Attenuation measurements conducted with a water-filled exposure vessel in or out of the sound beam indicated that a single thickness of the membrane reduced the peak positive/negative acoustic pressures by ∼2%. When sample rotation was desired, a variable speed motor was used to rotate the vessels at 200 rpm; rotation speed was monitored using an optical tachometer.

Ultrasound was produced by a focused 1-MHz transducer having a focal length of 5.1 cm, a resonance frequency of 1.02 MHz, and a −6 dB intensity beam diameter of 3.2 mm. Experimental samples were positioned at the focal plane of the transducer using a three-way positioner. For sample exposures, the peak positive and peak negative acoustic pressures were 6.2 and −3.6 MPa, respectively, corresponding to an SPTP intensity of ∼800 W/cm2. Pulse durations of 20, 200, 2000 or 20,000 μs were applied at a pulse repetition rate of 1 Hz. Pulse numbers of 1, 3, 5, 10, 50 or 100 pulses were applied to the exposed samples with or without sample rotation. Four experiments were conducted, each at a different pulse duration. An experiment was comprised of a set of n = 3 sham-exposed samples, and 12 sets of n = 3 exposed samples (1, 3, 5, 10, 50 or 100 pulses, ± sample rotation). Within each experiment, the hemolysis data obtained for the exposed samples were corrected for background (i.e., handling-related) hemolysis by subtraction of the mean hemolysis value for the shams from each experimental datum. The mean level of hemolysis in the sham-exposed samples was, thus, zero by definition.

A 0.5-mL aliquot of reconstituted red cells was mixed with 18 μL of Albunex® immediately prior to transfer of the sample to an exposure vessel. The vessel was then positioned in the ultrasound beam, rotation commenced where appropriate, and insonated as described above. Upon completion of the exposure/sham exposure treatment, the sample was removed from the vessel and transferred to a microcentrifuge tube. Following centrifugation, an aliquot of the supernatant was assayed spectrophotometrically for hemoglobin content. Osmotically-lysed samples of the stock suspensions were used as calibration standards; the blank was produced using cell-free autologous plasma.

Because the statistical hypothesis was that the various experimental treatments would produce levels of hemolysis greater than in the sham-exposed samples, and the latter were by definition set to zero (see above), one-sided, one-sample t-tests of Ha: HUS > 0 were used throughout, where HUS represents hemolysis in the ultrasound-exposed samples. These analyses were used to determine the least pulse duration × pulse number treatment combination required to induce statistically significant levels of hemolysis.

The complete datasets pertaining to these experiments are presented in tabular form in Tables 1 (with 200 rpm sample rotation) and 2 (stationary samples). The hemolysis data obtained using various pulse durations are plotted as a function of pulse number in Fig. 1A–D; the same data are plotted as a function of pulse duration at various pulse numbers in Fig. 2. Figure 1A–D demonstrates that, at all tested pulse durations, sonolysis of the erythrocytes was independent of sample rotation. Sonolysis increased with increasing pulse number at constant pulse duration. With 20-μs pulses, 50 pulses were required before statistically significant (i.e., greater than zero) hemolysis was observed (Tables 1, 2; Fig. 1A). At longer pulse durations (200, 2000 and 20,000 μs), a single pulse was sufficient to produce statistically significant levels of hemolysis (Tables 1, 2; Figs. 1B–D).

Figure 2 illustrates that, at constant pulse number, hemolysis scaled with increasing pulse duration. A single, 200-μs pulse induced a significant (p < 0.05), albeit low, level of hemolysis, with or without sample rotation. The results presented in Fig. 1A–D and Fig. 2 suggest that, as expected, hemolysis scales with the total duration of insonation. The hemolysis data from the four experimental regimens are co-plotted as a function of the total duration of insonation in Fig. 3, which demonstrates that equivalent hemolysis was produced by various pulse duration/pulse number combinations producing the same total exposure time, despite the different time courses of pulse application. Figure 3 indicates that a total insonation duration of ∼1 ms will lyse about 1% of the cells. If this relationship were to hold true for much higher pulse repetition frequencies—and it may not because of bubble growth/shrinkage/propagation effects—then these data suggest that an equivalent effect might arise from 1 s of total exposure to a sound field of comparable acoustic frequency and pressure amplitude using a pulse duration of 2 μs at 500-Hz pulse-repetition frequency (i.e., a duty factor of 0.001).

The data supported the a priori hypotheses that ultrasound-induced hemolysis would increase with increasing pulse duration, pulse number and total exposure duration. For example, with an increase in pulse number by a factor of ∼10 for the 2-ms pulse duration regimen, ultrasound-induced hemolysis increased by a factor of ∼10, irrespective of whether the exposure vessel was rotated or stationary (Fig. 1C). With increases in pulse duration at constant pulse number (Fig. 2), there were large increases in ultrasound-induced hemolysis (e.g., for 50 pulses, the extent of hemolysis for the 20, 200, 2000 and 20,000 μs pulse-duration regimens was 1, 10, 75 and 95%, respectively) (Fig. 2). These collective results suggest that initial cavitation events (e.g., involving the acoustic modification of the contrast agent microbubbles) are not the only contributors to cavitation activity and resultant cell damage but, rather, that the derivative gas bodies so produced can also contribute substantially to cavitation activity. Although the present experiments are insufficient to determine the relative contribution of these phenomena to total cell lysis, it is noteworthy that even were the initial acoustic modification of the contrast agent microbubbles to be completely without biological effect, relatively few derivative cavitation nuclei may be required to produce a substantial biological effect. From the results of experiments in which the initial concentration of cavitation nuclei was presumably very low and exposure vessels were rotated to facilitate recycling/re-nucleation of the host fluid, Miller and Williams (1989, 1992) concluded that one to a few bubbles may be sufficient to provide a stream of cavitation nuclei capable of undergoing subsequent inertial activation. This interpretation is also consistent with the observations of Brayman et al. (1997) and Dalecki et al. (1997a) Dalecki et al. (1997b)for an in vitro and an in vivo system, respectively, that remnants or derivatives of insonated Albunex® microspheres are capable of significantly increasing ultrasound-induced bioeffects. However, in the present experiments, the initial microbubble concentration (as stabilized contrast agent) was quite high and, presumably, the number of derivative gas bodies capable of undergoing acoustic cavitation was also relatively large. Evidence in support of this is provided by the observation that exposure vessel rotation during sample exposure to ultrasound had no effect on observed cell lysis.

The present data were derived from an experimental protocol designed to enhance the likelihood of cavitation-related bioeffect occurrence (e.g., the use of 1-MHz center frequency and an acoustic pressure in excess of 3 MPa are known to be lytically effective in the exposure system used for the present study) (Brayman et al. 1997). These exposure conditions have some similarities with, but also differ significantly in several aspects from, a typical diagnostic ultrasound exposure (e.g., an echocardiographic examination). Among the similarities are that the maximum total “treatment time” used here (100 s), and the total time used in contrast echocardiography may be similar, because 30–60 s is the approximate duration of contrast enhancement. The peak negative pressures used in echocardiography may also be comparable to those used here (Patton et al. 1994). Among the significant differences are that 20-μs ultrasound pulses were the shortest pulses used in the present in vitro experiments whereas, in diagnostic ultrasound examinations (exclusive of low amplitude, CW ultrasound), 20-μs pulses are about the maximum pulse duration used (pulse durations may be as long as 50 acoustic cycles in pulsed Doppler ultrasound; Duck et al. 1987). However, the total exposure duration in vivo is likely to be comparable to those used here because of the greater pulse-repetition frequencies used in imaging applications. For example, with a presumed pulse-repetition frequency of 1000 Hz, a pulse duration of 5 μs, and an examination time of 1 min, the total ultrasound “on” duration would about 0.3 s, an “on” time associated with ∼50% hemolysis in Fig. 3 for 1-MHz exposures. However, the center frequency used in imaging applications is higher than that used here (typically 2–3 MHz in echocardiography). Because ultrasound-induced hemolysis in this in vitro exposure system decreases very rapidly with increasing frequency (Brayman et al. 1997), it is expected that, for a “typical” 3-MHz ultrasound examination using contrast agents and an acoustic pressure of ∼3 MPa, any ultrasound-induced hemolysis produced may be below the limit of detection in samples drawn from the systemic circulation.

It is worth noting that ultrasound-induced bioeffects may scale in approximate proportion to the mechanical index (MI) of the output display standard (see, e.g., Miller and Thomas 1993; Brayman et al. 1997; Skyba et al. 1998) and, hence, it may be convenient (and tempting) to use the MI as a coarse predictor of potential bioeffect severity or extent. However, it bears emphasis that the MI is intended to predict the thresholds for inertial cavitation activity, and not to predict the extent of bioeffects which may be produced at supra-threshold acoustic pressures. The present results underscore this by demonstrating that pulse duration, pulse number and total exposure duration (at constant ultrasound frequency and acoustic pressures in these experiments) may be important determinants of ultrasound-induced bioeffects, with longer pulse durations and higher numbers of pulses associated with larger effects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by NIH Grant R37 CA39230-24.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Brayman AA, Miller MW. Acoustic cavitation nuclei survive the apparent ultrasonic destruction of Albunex® microspheres. Ultrasound Med Biol 1997;23:793–396.
